caption:
This fairytale town has heart shaped cutouts on the windows…and centuries ago, they weren’t just for decoration.

In medieval times, a heart carved into the wooden shutters meant a girl of marriageable age lived in that house.

No heart usually meant the “listing” was taken or the family wasn’t open to offers 🤫

This is…

📍 Colmar, France

Save this if you’re single and booking flights immediately 😅😂

This colorful town on the Italian Riviera was once a separate island before nature slowly connected it back to mainland Italy over time.

That natural formation created two completely different bays on each side of town.

One is called the Bay of Silence with calm water, tiny fishing boats, pastel reflections, and peaceful atmosphere.

The other side is lively, social, and full of boats, aperitivo spots, restaurants, etc.

This is…

📍 Sestri Levante, Italy

🚗 How to get here
Sestri Levante is easily accessible by train from Genoa, Milan, Pisa, or Florence and the historic center is walking distance from the station.

🍝 What to eat
Try fresh pesto pasta, Ligurian focaccia, seafood linguine, and sunset aperitivo by the water.

📌 Save this for your Italy trip

caption:
I never grew up thinking, “One day, I’ll be a content creator.” 

👩‍💻I was supposed to follow the safe path.
I went to school, got my degree, landed my 9-5 job in the advanced air mobility industry and I’m VERY proud of that. I get to be part of very few people shaping the future of air mobility. 

But deep down… I always felt this quiet pull towards something more. I always find myself chasing little pockets of the world that most people skip or haven’t even heard of! The places not trending on Instagram and tiny villages where you get lost on purpose.

So I picked up my camera 📸 and started sharing stories through not just places, but emotions. Slowly, my small corner of Instagram grew into a beautiful community of people who are always looking for something unique or different.

✅ I want to be a content creator to inspire 
people who feel stuck, 
people who think travel isn’t for them,
people who think they need to quit their jobs to see the world. 
You don’t. I didn’t.
✅ You can chase your dreams without sacrificing stability.
✅ You can start before you’re “ready.”
‼️ You can live fully even if the world tells you to choose one path.

It’s possible to build a life where one day, I can turn this into something even bigger ✈️ my own business, my own brand, my own freedom. Remember, “you don’t need to be great to start but you NEED TO start in order to be great”. 

and if one person watches my content and thinks “Wait… maybe I can do this too”..that’s the real win

caption:
‼️and the gardens here inspired one of the most famous composers in history 🤯

🤍 Follow for more unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

This medieval villa was once one of the largest and most expensive estates on the entire Amalfi Coast.

It was built in the 1200s by the wealthy Rufolo family, and was designed to show power, status, and wealth at a time when Ravello was an important trading town connected to the Mediterranean world.

Today, parts of the villa are still in ruins. You walk through Moorish arches, stone towers, hidden courtyards, and gardens hanging above the sea.

This is…

📍 Villa Rufolo, Ravello, Amalfi Coast, Italy

🚗 How to get here
The easiest way is by car or private driver from Amalfi, Positano, or Salerno. Ravello sits high above the coastline so expect winding mountain roads with incredible views the entire way.

🎟️ Things to know
The villa is famous for its cliffside gardens and terrace overlooking the Amalfi Coast. If you visit in summer, Ravello festival concerts are sometimes held here

caption:
‼️if you are visiting Venice this year👇🏻 

🤍 Follow for more unique places and travel guides around the world

Getting there

Fly into Venice Marco Polo Airport. From there, you can take a water taxi (expensive but iconic) or the Alilaguna water bus (budget-friendly). 

If you’re coming by train, Venezia Santa Lucia drops you straight into the heart of the city.

Where to stay

If it’s your first time, stay near San Marco or along the Grand Canal so you can walk everywhere early in the morning before crowds take over.

🤍 I stayed at the @stregisvenice and it was an epic experience. One of my favorite moments of staying here was that every evening they carry on a ritual called champagne sabering - where a bottle is opened with a sword (yes, an actual sword) and I actually got to do it myself and then try it 🤍

Things to do

Get lost. Not even kidding! Venice is best experienced with no plan
Wake up early and walk through Piazza San Marco before 8 AM
Take a vaporetto or gondola ride down the Grand Canal
Visit Burano for colorful houses or Murano for glassmaking
Climb the Campanile for one of the best views in the city

What to eat

Skip the tourist places right by the main squares.
Walk a few streets away and look for smaller, local spots.
Try cicchetti (Venetian tapas), fresh pasta, and tiramisu (this is where it was born).
Also, don’t leave without having a spritz by the water.

Things no one tells you ⚠️

Venice is beautiful, but it’s also crowded. Early mornings and late evenings are your best friend
Restaurants near major attractions are often overpriced and not worth it
Pack light! There are a LOT of bridges and no Ubers here
Acqua alta (flooding) can happen, especially in certain months, so check ahead

caption:
‼️The Tuscany road trip you will think about forever👇🏻

🤍 Follow for more unique places and road trips around the world @tripuntraveled

This is the route I would actually do if you want medieval towers, hilltop towns, wine villages, cypress roads, Renaissance streets, and countryside views.

📍Volterra
Start here if you want Tuscany with an ancient, mysterious feeling. This town has Etruscan roots, a Roman theatre, medieval streets, and alabaster shops that make it feel completely different

📍San Gimignano
This is the famous medieval Manhattan of Tuscany, with 14 surviving towers still rising above the town. Go early or later in the day because this one gets busy, but the skyline is absolutely worth seeing once in your life

📍Certaldo Alto
This is one of the most underrated stops on this route. It is small, brick colored, quiet, and tied to Giovanni Boccaccio, the author of The Decameron. Take the funicular up

📍Montalcino
Come here for Brunello wine and fortress views. This is a good stop for wine tasting or a long countryside meal.

📍San Quirico d’Orcia
Beautiful and unreal close to the famous cypress trees, Chapel of Madonna di Vitaleta, Horti Leonini, and some of the most iconic Val d’Orcia landscapes.

📍Pienza
End here for the soft, golden, romantic version of Tuscany. Pienza was redesigned as an ideal Renaissance town, and will make you fall in love with Tuscany.

🚗 How to plan this route
Do not try to squeeze all of this into one rushed day unless you enjoy suffering beautifully.

Day 1 - Volterra, San Gimignano, Certaldo
Day 2 - Montalcino, San Quirico d’Orcia, Pienza

But if you have more time, make it 3 days and stay overnight in the countryside.

📌 Save this for your Tuscany trip because this route gives you the best mix of medieval towns, wine country, cypress views, and more.

In this tiny Umbrian town, there is an alley called Vicolo Baciadonne, which translates to kiss the women.

caption:
‼️Venice - overrated or unforgettable?👇🏻

I finally understand why people either LOVE Venice or absolutely hate it.

But I have to say, those who hate it might be doing this -
they arrive midday
fight crowds around Rialto and San Marco
eat at tourist restaurants
leave a few hours later
and then wonder what the hype was about 🤔

But the biggest Venice hack is to STAY OVERNIGHT

Where to stay?
The San Marco / Grand Canal area for classic luxury Venice and iconic views
Cannaregio for quieter canals and local feel
Dorsoduro for artsy, romantic Venice

Most underrated experience?
Taking a private taxi down the Grand Canal at sunset

For a special experience?
Book one nice aperitivo or dinner with canal views instead of standing in lines. Atmosphere matters in Venice

Beautiful stays worth looking into
@stregisvenice
@amanvenice
@thegrittipalace
@belmondhotelcipriani

What surprised me the most?
Venice doesn’t feel impressive because of one landmark. It feels impressive because an entire city lives on water.

Then there are tiny bookstores, hidden courtyards, quiet canals, faded buildings, laundry hanging above the water…it feels more like a film set than a city sometimes.

📍 Venice, Italy

caption:

🤍 Follow for more unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

This tiny Tuscan town was completely redesigned in the 1400s by Pope Pius II to become the perfect city.

Every street, every building, every open square was planned with symmetry, perspective, and balance in mind.

The main square opens up like a stage…
the cathedral, palace, and town hall are perfectly aligned, and the streets are designed to frame the rolling hills of Val d’Orcia like a painting.

This is…

📍 Pienza, Tuscany, Italy

🚗 How to get here
It’s best to visit as part of a Val d’Orcia road trip. You can also take a train to nearby towns like Montepulciano and drive from there

🧀 What to try
Pecorino di Pienza
Fresh pasta with local cheese
Anything simple…the quality here is phenomenal

📌 Save this for your Tuscany trip because this one is indeed perfect

ownerFullName: PALAK & VAIBHAV | Travel and Adventure Couple

caption:
‼️location👇🏻 

🤍 Follow for more unique places and roadtrips around the world @tripuntraveled

Walking through this place honestly felt overstimulating in the best possible way.

Your eyes don’t know where to focus first because every wall, ceiling, archway, and courtyard is layered with insane detail. Arabic calligraphy flows across the walls, reflections through the water, and sunlight changes the colors of the stone

What makes it even crazier is that much of this was built in the 1200s by the Nasrid dynasty during Moorish rule in Spain…nearly 800 years ago.

and despite wars, conquests, and centuries of history, it still feels impossibly ahead of its time.

This is…

📍 Alhambra, Granada, Spain

🎟️ Tips before visiting
Book your tickets weeks in advance because they regularly sell out, especially for the Nasrid Palaces.

Don’t miss
The Court of the Lions, the intricate ceilings inside the Nasrid Palaces, and the Generalife gardens with flowing water channels and views over Granada.

📌 Save this for your Spain trip

Have you ever seen architecture this detailed before?

ownerFullName: PALAK & VAIBHAV | Travel and Adventure Couple

caption:
This is the most beautiful Cherry Blossom destination in USA 🌸😍 

✨For more of such content with amazing destinations and travel guides, follow @peekinourjournal and plan your next trip 🫶

Every year during spring, the cherry blossoms fully bloom and bear incredibly gorgeous flowers. These cotton like flowers with hues of pink line up along the streets of Washington DC and completely transform the city 🥹

The entire capital of USA is filled with these trees and here are our favorite spots to see them 😍

🗼 Tidal Basin- The most popular spot in DC for Cherry Blossoms and for good reason! It is absolutely gorgeous but super crowded
⛲️ Enid A Haupt Garden- A stunning garden located in the heart of the city. You can see a ton of colorful Magnolias here
🚴‍♂️ Hains Point/East Potomac Park- The cherry blossoms line up the entire road here. Beautiful spot for outdoor acrivities like picnic, cycling etc
🏛️ United States Capitol- Located at the end of National Mall, it’s a great spot to see some Cherry Blossoms and enjoy views of this beautiful building
🪦 Oak Hill Cemetery- Located in Georgetown near Dumbarton Oaks, this is a beautiful place to see the blossoms. Please be respectful during your visit
👨‍⚖️ Congressional Cemetery- Located on the east side of DC, this site is operated by the national park service and is a great spot to see Okame cherry blossoms. Again, important to be respectful if you visit

.
.
.
.
